summary: Opaque Windows native binary auto-loaded on require() in package advertised as zero-dependency
details: On every require()/import of agentsync-pkg, src/index.js (lines 142-150) silently loads bin/native/parser.node — a 2.9 MB Windows PE binary — using `require(p)` wrapped in a try/catch that swallows all errors. Loading a.node file executes arbitrary native machine code in the consumer's Node.js process. The package's surrounding context actively misdirects auditors away from this binary: the README claims 'Zero dependencies. Nothing to audit, nothing to get compromised in a supply-chain attack' and displays a 'dependencies-0' badge, while the JavaScript markdown-sync logic in render.js/targets.js uses plain regex/string operations and has no functional need for a native parser. package.json declares cross-platform support (win32/linux/darwin × x64/arm64) but only a Windows x64 PE is shipped, and the nested try/catch ensures the discrepancy fails silently on macOS/Linux so install-time testing on those platforms reveals nothing. Author metadata (noreply@agentsync.dev) is placeholder-shaped. The combination of opaque native code execution on import, a cover-story filename ('parser' for a tool that does not parse), documentation that explicitly denies any binary content, and error-swallowing concealment matches the opaque-native-binary dropper pattern. The PE's behavior cannot be verified from source.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
